---
title: "2c Activity: Python Project Management"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    css: _materials.css
    include-after-body: _progress.html
---


## Goal

We will add a small `pyproject.toml` file to the same practice repository, list a few packages, install them, pin the versions we care about, and test the environment in a notebook.

Note: The checkboxes on this page are only stored locally in your browser. They are not submitted or synced anywhere.

::: {.guide-progress}

Progress: <span data-progress-count>0</span> / <span data-progress-total>0</span> steps complete.

<button class="reset-progress" type="button" data-progress-reset>Reset progress</button>

:::


## Activity

::: {.panel-tabset}

## 1. Open The Practice Repo

Open the `python_tmr_practice` repository in Codespaces.

This should be the same repository you used for `2a` and `2b`.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-open-practice-repo">
  <span>I opened the practice repository in Codespaces.</span>
</label>

:::


## 2. Create `pyproject.toml`

In the Explorer, create a file named `pyproject.toml` at the top level of the repository.

There are tools that can generate a starter `pyproject.toml`, such as `uv init`, Poetry, and Hatch. For this activity, we will write a very small one ourselves so we can see exactly what each piece does.

Add this starter content:

```toml
[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project]
name = "python-tmr-practice"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.13"
dependencies = [
    "ipykernel",
    "nbformat",
    "pandas",
    "plotly",
    "polars",
]

[tool.setuptools]
py-modules = []
```

Save the file.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-create-pyproject">
  <span>I created and saved `pyproject.toml`.</span>
</label>

:::


## 3. Read The File

Notice the pieces:

- `name`: the project name;
- `version`: a project version;
- `requires-python`: the Python version expectation;
- `dependencies`: Python packages the project needs;
- `tool.setuptools`: a small setting that says this practice project does not define an importable Python package.

Right now the dependency list names the packages we want, without requiring exact versions. This file stores the project configuration, but it does not install anything just by existing.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-read-pyproject">
  <span>I can identify the dependency list in `pyproject.toml`.</span>
</label>

:::


## 4. Install From The Project

Open a terminal and run:

```bash
python -m pip install -e .
```

This installs from the project file. Since the dependency list does not specify exact versions yet, `pip` will choose current compatible versions.

Then check that Polars imports:

```bash
python -c "import polars as pl; print(pl.__version__)"
```

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-install-from-pyproject">
  <span>I installed from `pyproject.toml` with `python -m pip install -e .`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-import-polars">
  <span>I confirmed that Polars imports.</span>
</label>

:::


## 5. Pin The Versions We Care About

After installing, ask Python what is installed:

```bash
python -m pip freeze
```

The terminal will print a long list of installed packages. We are using this as a readout, not saving it to a file.

Find the lines for the packages we intentionally installed:

```text
ipykernel==...
nbformat==...
pandas==...
plotly==...
polars==...
```

Edit the `dependencies` part of `pyproject.toml` so it looks like this, using the version numbers from your terminal:

```toml
dependencies = [
    "ipykernel==...",
    "nbformat==...",
    "pandas==...",
    "plotly==...",
    "polars==...",
]
```

Do not copy every package from `pip freeze`. Many of those are packages that our chosen packages need. The installer can solve those later.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-run-pip-freeze">
  <span>I ran `python -m pip freeze` in the terminal.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-find-package-versions">
  <span>I copied the package versions I care about into `pyproject.toml`.</span>
</label>

:::


## 6. Reinstall From The Pinned Project

The file now records the packages this project needs and the versions we want to keep using.

Run:

```bash
python -m pip install -e .
```

In this Codespace, most of the work may already be done. The important point is that `pyproject.toml` is now a recipe that a future environment can use.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-reinstall-from-pinned-pyproject">
  <span>I reinstalled from the pinned `pyproject.toml`.</span>
</label>

:::


## 7. Test In A Notebook

Create a new notebook named `environment_check.ipynb`.

If VS Code asks you to choose a kernel, choose the Python environment for this Codespace.

In the first code cell, paste and run:

```python
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import polars as pl

print("Polars:", pl.__version__)
print("pandas:", pd.__version__)
```

In a second code cell, paste and run:

```python
sample_pl = pl.DataFrame(
    {
        "year": [2022, 2023, 2024],
        "projects": [1, 2, 4],
    }
)

sample_pd = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "year": [2022, 2023, 2024],
        "projects": [1, 2, 4],
    }
)

total_projects = sample_pl.select(pl.col("projects").sum()).item()
print("Total projects:", total_projects)

fig = px.line(
    sample_pd,
    x="year",
    y="projects",
    markers=True,
)
fig.show()
```

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-create-notebook">
  <span>I created `environment_check.ipynb`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-run-import-cell">
  <span>I ran a notebook cell that imports Polars, pandas, and Plotly.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-run-plot-cell">
  <span>I ran a notebook cell that creates a small Plotly figure.</span>
</label>

:::


## 8. Commit And Push

Use Source Control:

1. Review the changes.
1. Find `pyproject.toml` and `environment_check.ipynb` under **Changes**.
1. Hover over each file and click the **+** button to stage it.
1. Confirm that the files moved from **Changes** to **Staged Changes**.
1. Commit with a message such as:

```text
Added Python project environment files.
```

Push or sync the commit to GitHub.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-review-environment-diff">
  <span>I reviewed the environment file changes.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-commit-environment-files">
  <span>I committed the environment files.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="2c-push-pyproject">
  <span>I pushed or synced the commit to GitHub.</span>
</label>

:::

:::
